params.title: This Is KurimaSense. The End Of Farming On *Guesswork*.
params.kicker: Satellite field intelligence for Zimbabwean farmers. See what your crop is doing while you can still act on it.

slides.alt_text: A farmer ploughs red soil with two white oxen under a storm-lit sky. Large yellow serif text reads: This is KurimaSense. The end of farming on guesswork.

params.title:
Your Field Tells
You Too Late.
params.body:
By the time a leaf curls or a corner yellows, the yield decision is already made.

Guesswork means finding out at the weighbridge — when nothing can be changed.

slides.alt_text: A green tractor beside a tall maize stand under a brooding sky. Text reads: your field tells you too late. Guesswork means finding out at the weighbridge, when nothing can be changed.

params.title:
One Guess For
Twenty Hectares.
params.body:
Inputs spread evenly and hoped over. The sliding corner scouted on foot, after it shows.

The field is never uniform — guesswork treats it like it is.

slides.alt_text: Tomato rows inside a tunnel greenhouse. Text reads: one guess for twenty hectares. The field is never uniform, but guesswork treats it like it is.

params.title:
See It Early.
Act While It Counts.
params.body:
Satellites read crop stress *10 to 15 days* before your eye can.

That head start is the difference between saving the stand and counting the loss.

slides.alt_text: A farmer swings a hoe over wet ground, reflected in the water. Text reads: see it early, act while it counts. Satellites read crop stress 10 to 15 days before your eye can.

slides.alt_text: A green tractor beside a maize stand. Three columns explain what KurimaSense does: reads every field from above as the satellite passes; alerts you the moment a block starts to slip; scores each field with one honest number, the KurimaScore.

params.question: Ready to stop *guessing* — and start seeing?
params.cta: FOLLOW KURIMASENSE — THE STAKES COME NEXT

slides.alt_text: A young woman farmer in a straw hat leans on a wooden rail, looking ahead. Text asks: ready to stop guessing and start seeing? A yellow banner says follow KurimaSense, the stakes come next.

caption:
This is KurimaSense.

We sell one thing: the end of farming on guesswork. Satellite field intelligence for Zimbabwean farmers — smallholder and commercial — that tells you what your crop is actually doing, while there is still time to act on it.

Here is the problem we exist for. By the time a leaf curls or a corner yellows, the yield decision has already been made. Guesswork means spreading inputs evenly and hoping, scouting trouble on foot after it shows, and learning the real cost at the weighbridge.

Here is the shift. Satellites read crop stress 10 to 15 days before the eye can. In the app, every field carries one honest number — the KurimaScore, 0 to 100 — refreshed as the satellite passes, with an alert the moment a block starts to slip.

We are not here to "bring tech to the farm". You know your land. We hand you a sharper instrument, and you still make the call.

This is the first of a short series. Next: what farming blind actually costs — with the numbers.

Follow along, and save this for someone still farming on guesswork.
first_comment: The 10-to-15-day early-detection window is from a 2025 Frontiers in Plant Science review of crop-stress sensing. We never invent numbers — every figure we post carries its source, and we will always show it.
rationale: Series opener, rebuilt as an explicit brand introduction: this is KurimaSense, this is the problem, this is what the app does. Arc runs pain (find out too late; one guess for twenty hectares) → benefit (see stress 10–15 days early, sourced) → the product itself (icon-row: reads, alerts, scores) → a follow CTA that hands the viewer into the series. Product is introduced at a high level here; post 3 goes deep on the KurimaScore. The one hard number is sourced; anti-saviour stance kept (market law). British spelling, Zimbabwe furniture (weighbridge, blocks, hectares, scouting).
